exemplar.title: Organizational Tracking Management and Budget System

detailedExemplarData.title: Organizational Tracking Management and Budget System

strengths: Clear, realistic scenario with identified client
Well-justified rationale linking technology choices to client needs
Comprehensive, measurable success criteria covering all key functions
weaknesses: Lacks primary evidence of client consultation (interview transcripts, emails)
Minor repetition in rationale reduces clarity
One success criterion is vague without quantifiable metrics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning section demonstrates strong justification for the product choice and an extensive set of success criteria, warranting a high band score. However, evidence of client consultation is only second-hand, limiting the scenario strand to mid-band. Balancing these yields a score of 5/6.

strengths: Moderate complexity: dynamic ID generation, error-handled CRUD operations, custom stack-based calculator
Effective, appropriate use of Python, PostgreSQL, CTkinter, SQL joins and error dialogs
Thoughtful UI error-handler enhancements improving user experience
weaknesses: No bibliographic citations or source attribution for external libraries
Lacks deeper explanation of session lifecycle and advanced data-structure code examples
Minor data inconsistencies (e.g., stray character in sample data)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The development work shows solid complexity and mature tool use, but missing source citations and limited depth in certain technical explanations prevent it from reaching the top band. A score of 7/12 reflects these strengths and gaps.

strengths: Comprehensive design overview with wireframes, flowcharts and ERD
Test plan links most success criteria to actions and expected results
weaknesses: Test plan omits boundary and abnormal cases and lacks pass/fail metrics in many rows
Some UI mock-ups are text-heavy or incomplete, missing callouts
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the design overview is detailed and the test plan broadly covers criteria, the absence of any record-of-tasks and incomplete test entries are major omissions. This mixture of strong and missing elements aligns with a mid-band score of 3/6.

strengths: Core pages function as described in screenshots
Database schema supports future data expansion
weaknesses: No demonstration video to confirm functionality
Key features (e.g., reminders) are not yet implemented
Tight coupling between GUI code and SQL hinders straightforward extensibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Partial product functionality is evident, but missing video proof and incomplete features keep it in the middle band for functionality. The design allows extension but requires significant effort, fitting the 2/4 band.

strengths: Evaluation addresses each success criterion with pass/fail statements
Recommendations are realistic and tied to identified shortcomings
Some client feedback is summarized and incorporated
weaknesses: Lacks supporting evidence (annotated screenshots, logs) for evaluation claims
Minimal client/adviser feedback without iterative cycles or transcripts
Recommendations lack prioritization and cost/benefit justification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows a structured review against criteria and sensible recommendations. However, limited evidence and superficial client feedback keep this in the mid-band. A score of 3/6 accurately reflects these mixed elements.

strandSelections.reasoning: The solution overview contains wire-frame style GUI mock-ups, three detailed flowcharts, an ERD and an extensive data dictionary.  Together these artefacts demonstrate structure, navigation and data relationships comprehensively, clearly mapping out the full development process.

The rationale explains why a database-driven, Python/CTk/PostgreSQL solution is appropriate, referencing security, scalability, relevant libraries and the clientâs need for centralisation.  The justification directly links platform choices to success criteria, showing a full argument.  Minor repetition prevents a perfect 6, but overall the explanation is fully justified.

Python, PostgreSQL, CTkinter, SQL joins, try/except wrappers and custom error dialogs are used effectively and appropriately.  The integration of GUI, database and security features indicates mature tool use, warranting entry into the highest band; however, limited automation/documentation suggests the lower end of that band.

A table links most success criteria to specific test actions and expected results, showing black-box focus.  While some rows are incomplete and the plan omits boundary/abnormal tests, the outline is broadly sufficient, fitting the 3-4 band.

Three recommendations (more filters, reminders, permission levels) are realistic and linked to identified shortcomings, but justification is brief.  That meets the descriptor for âlargely realisticâ but lacks the depth expected for the highest band.

The student explains techniques (arrays, stacks, hash maps) but provides no bibliographic citation for any external sources or libraries.  Some explanation is present, so the work fits the 1-4 band rather than zero, but cannot reach the higher band that requires full source attribution.
No chronological record-of-tasks table is included.  Development steps are discussed in prose, but the rubric explicitly requires a dated task list; hence this strand scores zero.

The code demonstrates multiple tables, relational links, dynamic ID generation, error-handled CRUD operations and a stack-based calculator.  These features show a moderate degree of complexity and inventiveness beyond basic CRUD but fall short of advanced algorithms or sophisticated UI patterns expected for the top band.

Fourteen clear, measurable and relevant criteria span usability, security, data manipulation and persistence, covering all major functions expected by the client.  They are specific enough to test and therefore constitute a comprehensive range of appropriate criteria.

Only a short general client comment and a summary table are included; there are no signed forms, interview transcripts or iterative feedback cycles.  This represents limited feedback, fitting the 1-2 band.

Tables are normalised and IDs auto-generated, implying the structure could be extended, but tight coupling of GUI code with SQL means modification would require noticeable effort.  Expansion is possible but not straightforward, matching the 1-2 band top mark.

The student clearly states a realistic scenario (non-profit needing a centralised information system) and identifies a named representative (Ms Y).  However, evidence of consultation is limited to second-hand quotes; there are no minutes, e-mails or screenshots of interviews to demonstrate a âthoroughly describedâ dialogue.  This aligns best with the middle band where some consultation is apparent but not detailed.

Screenshots and descriptions suggest the core pages operate, but no video evidence is provided and several functions (e.g., reminders) are pending.  Functionality therefore appears partial rather than fully demonstrated, placing it in the middle band.

The student evaluates each success criterion and states whether it was met, but depth is limited to brief statements without supporting evidence such as annotated screenshots or user-acceptance results.  This corresponds to a partial evaluation.

annotations.comment: Figure 3âs caption is correctly labeled and provides context for the main page UI, aiding the readerâs understanding of functionality.
Budget Page UI in Figure 4 is comprehensive, showing multiple states in one table. It effectively demonstrates data presentation and user controls.
The Add User Interface description (Figure 4) is too text-heavy and lacks clear callouts for user actions. Add numbered annotations to link UI elements to descriptions.
The main pages flowchart is well-drawn and labeled (Figure 7), clearly illustrating navigation logic.
Only one high-level flowchart appears; sub-processes for login, budget operations and error handling are missing. Include detailed flowcharts for these to fully map processes.
The ERD (page 11) comprehensively shows entities and relationships. Tables and cardinalities are clear, supporting the data model.

The scenario section outlines the clientâs issues well, but lacks primary evidence of consultation such as interview transcripts, emails or meeting minutes. Adding concrete artifacts would demonstrate thorough client engagement.
The rationale for choosing Python/CTk/PostgreSQL is well justified, linking security, scalability and library support directly to client needs. This shows a comprehensive product justification.
Consider reducing repetition in the rationale section; for instance, consolidate explanations of security features into one coherent argument for clearer justification.
The success criteria list is extensive and measurable, covering usability, security and data management comprehensively. This provides a strong basis for testing.
Success criterion 3 (ânavigation based on specific tasksâ) is vague. Specify measurable metrics (e.g., response time or number of clicks) to make testing more precise.
Boundary and abnormal test cases (e.g., empty inputs, invalid credentials) are not listed under success criterion 14. Include these to ensure robustness.
The Home/Notes UI mock-up is clear and includes annotations. This demonstrates a solid design overview for user interaction.
The login credentials form mock-up contains many empty cells and lacks expected/actual result fields. Simplify and complete the layout to better communicate design intent.
The multi-panel interface design in Criterion B is ambitious, but the Form/2 artifact is overly dense. Break it into separate diagrams for add/edit/delete flows to improve clarity.
The Notes/Home flowchart caption is present but the flowchart itself is omitted on page 10. Ensure the graphic appears to avoid reader confusion.
The data dictionary includes key tables but omits several (e.g., Donator, Note). Add missing tables or reference the appendix for completeness.
Source citations for table definitions (e.g., psycopg2 docs) are absent. Include bibliographic references for external techniques to meet higher documentation standards.
The test plan table is thorough, but many rows have no explicit pass/fail criteria. Add quantifiable expected outcomes (e.g., â100% data retrievalâ) for clarity.
There is no dated record-of-tasks table. Adding a chronological task log with dates and responsibilities would strengthen process transparency.
The login snippet shows function but lacks discussion of session lifecycle. Expanding on authentication flow would demonstrate deeper technical understanding.
The error handler UI illustration is clear and shows custom messages effectively. This demonstrates thoughtful use of tools to improve user experience.
The explanation of arrays for page-frame storage is high-level. Adding code examples of array operations would illustrate deeper mastery of data structures.
ResultsStack history table includes a stray character âdâ in the last cell, indicating a data inconsistency. Clean up sample data to accurately reflect intended behavior.
No video evidence of functionality is provided. Including a short demo video would better demonstrate product functionality and confirm test results.
Evaluation table lists pass/fail but lacks supporting evidence such as annotated screenshots or log outputs. Incorporate visual proofs to substantiate the evaluation.
Client feedback is high-level (âuseful but can do moreâ). Gather iterative feedback from multiple stakeholders for richer evaluation insights.
Recommendations are realistic but lack prioritization and cost/benefit rationale. Rank suggestions and justify resource estimates to strengthen future planning.
